ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai crispy chicken dry noodle. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle dishes) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for Thai crispy chicken dry noodle.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Thai noodle preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of dry Thai crispy chicken noodle is approximately 380 g, comprising a portion of egg noodles topped with deep-fried crispy chicken pieces, seasoned with soy-based sauce and garnished with spring onion.

nutrient_source.title: Noodle dish with meat (chicken), dry style — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition reference for Thai noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Dry Thai Crispy Chicken Noodle' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 172030), deep-fried chicken (FDC ID 171477), and soy-based seasoning components, weighted to a representative 100 g portion of the assembled dish (approximately 50% noodle, 35% crispy chicken, 15% sauce/garnish). FAO/INFOODS SEA regional food composition data for Thai noodle dishes used as a cross-reference for plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of noodle-based dishes — egg noodles cooked, analogous estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dry Thai Crispy Chicken Noodle. GI of 55 is estimated based on the GI of cooked egg noodles (GI ~52–57, low category) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The presence of protein from crispy chicken and fat from frying oil is expected to modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (55 × 85.5 g available carbohydrate per 380 g serving) / 100 = 47.0, rounded to 47.2 accounting for sauce contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1824 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as a single serving provides approximately 79% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, primarily from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) and high glycemic load per serving (~47)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in reduced portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the high glycemic load from egg noodles can cause significant blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit frequency of consumption due to the high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from deep-fried chicken, both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Thai Crispy Chicken Noodle is a Thai-style noodle dish consisting of springy egg noodles served in a dry (non-soup) format, topped with deep-fried crispy chicken pieces and seasoned with a soy and oyster sauce-based dressing, garlic, and spring onion. The dish is characterised by a contrast of textures — the crunchy, golden exterior of the fried chicken against the soft, chewy noodles — and a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile. It is commonly served in Thai restaurants in Singapore, including at Saap Saap Thai outlets. Nutritionally, a standard restaurant serving (~380 g) provides approximately 752 kcal, 42.6 g protein, 29.6 g fat, and 85.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1824 mg per serving) due to soy and oyster sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low glycemic index (~55) and high glycemic load (~47) per serving require portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or shellfish allergies. It is dairy-free. The crispy chicken component contributes meaningful protein and B vitamins, while the egg noodle base provides the primary carbohydrate energy source.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Dry Thai Crispy Chicken Noodle was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked egg noodles and deep-fried chicken, weighted by estimated dish composition. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked egg noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this dish: USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 172030) and deep-fried chicken (FDC ID 171477) do not provide a consistent or reliable vitamin D value for the assembled dish, and FAO/INFOODS SEA does not report vitamin D for this food category. All other -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been replaced with estimated numeric values derived from USDA FoodData Central component data and are approximate. The halal status is listed as false because Saap Saap Thai is not certified halal as of the processing date; consumers should verify directly with the restaurant. The food_group is retained as 'Meat and alternatives' from the input record, consistent with the dish being protein-anchored by chicken. GI source is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for cooked egg noodles (GI ~55). Serving size of 380 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant plate; actual weight may vary by restaurant.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls into the 'low' category (<=55) per University of Sydney GI Database definitions.
Updated gi_source.source_match_notes, health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor, and llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect 'low glycemic index' for consistency.

Iteration 3: Expanded the notes field to explicitly document that vitamin_d_mcg = -1 because it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries (FDC ID 172030 and FDC ID 171477) used for this dish, and FAO/INFOODS SEA does not report vitamin D for this food category. This satisfies the g3_numeric_hygiene sentinel value documentation requirement identified in the iteration 2 validator feedback.
